result.lens: Research Integrity Pre-Registration Review — Single-Operator Independence Model

findings.title: RFC-3161 Timestamping Promised but Not Implemented
findings.where: PROJECT_CHARTER.md §8.5, PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md, ADR-0007, implementation status
findings.problem: The charter claims 'timestamped pre-registration — every hypothesis the platform pursues is logged to the Preregistration Ledger with an RFC 3161 timestamp before confirmatory testing begins' as a critical independence mechanism. The PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md documents a detailed RFC-3161 procedure including openssl/FreeTSA integration. However, no Python implementation of RFC-3161 timestamping exists in src/. The charter explicitly states this mechanism is required for the credible operation of the single-operator model. A journal reviewer or FDA-style audit would request the .tsr files as evidence of the timestamps — they do not exist. This undermines the entire claimed independence structure for a single-operator research platform.
findings.evidence: (1) PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md §'RFC 3161 Timestamp Procedure' documents manual openssl/FreeTSA workflow; (2) No grep hits for RFC-3161, TSA, or timestamp service implementation in src/ codebase; (3) docs/protocol/registration/ directory does not exist; (4) gate_coordinator_node() uses datetime.datetime.now() (UTC ISO strings) instead of cryptographic timestamps; (5) Charter §8.5 lists RFC-3161 as a core independence mechanism alongside code gates and append-only provenance
findings.recommendation: Before any data analysis begins: implement RFC-3161 timestamping as an actual operational gate. Either (a) integrate a Python RFC-3161 library (pyasn1-modules is in venv) and automate the FreeTSA workflow at hypothesis registration/gate approval, or (b) explicitly demote RFC-3161 to 'post-publication best practice' and revise the charter to acknowledge that Gate 2 decisions rely on checkpoint logs (PostgreSQL timestamps) rather than external TSA signatures. Option (a) is stronger for a publishable, auditable study. The current state (documented but unimplemented) is a critical integrity gap. A reviewer will ask: 'Where are the .tsr files?' and the answer 'they were never created' fails a credibility test.

findings.title: Operator Sees Exploratory Results Before Gate 2 Hypothesis Lock — Bias Vector Not Mitigated
findings.where: Charter §8.5, Gate 2 architecture, hypothesis_formalizer agent, ADR-0012 (autonomous architecture), ADR-0014 (escalation via GitHub/Claude)
findings.problem: The core claim is that single-operator bias is controlled via 'structural transparency' and code gates. However, the hypothesis formalizer agent is explicitly designed to 'receive EDA summary from EDA agent' and 'formalize each research question into H[module].[n] format' based on exploratory findings. Thomas (the single operator) sees the EDA summaries, then approves the hypotheses at Gate 2. The charter acknowledges this dynamic explicitly: 'The Gate 2 proposal contains the formalized hypothesis set. The operator approves the hypothesis set as part of Gate 2.' This means the operator can (and likely will) see exploratory results before forming/approving the hypotheses that will test them. The independence claim rests on the assertion that this is structurally mitigated by 'code gates' and 'append-only provenance,' but neither prevents the operator from seeing data-dependent hypotheses form in front of them and approving them. The hypothesis formalizer's _validate_no_harking method is a no-op: it logs a warning if exploratory p-values match hypothesis direction but does not block the addition. This is not structural independence — it is procedural hope.
findings.evidence: (1) hypothesis_formalizer/agent.py: 'Receive EDA summary from EDA agent' and 'formalize each research question into H[module].[n] format' from exploratory findings; (2) Gate 2 checklist in gates.py explicitly lists 'Hypothesis set complete — every confirmatory hypothesis in PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md' and 'Effect size thresholds are grounded in domain literature — not selected to match the EDA distribution (that would be HARKing)' — the checklist is a post-hoc procedural control, not a structural barrier; (3) _validate_no_harking (line 121–141) does not raise on HARKing detection; it only logs warnings for human review; (4) Charter §8.5: 'Timestamped pre-registration — every hypothesis the platform pursues is logged to the Preregistration Ledger with an RFC 3161 timestamp before confirmatory testing begins' — but the hypothesis formalizer produces those hypotheses AFTER seeing exploratory data, so the timestamp proves registration-after-exploration, not independence; (5) ADR-0012 states 'Thomas approves hard pivots; the platform handles everything else' — Thomas is the only human in the loop, seeing all exploratory summaries before Gate 2
findings.recommendation: This is a fundamental tension in the design. The current mitigation is insufficient for a journal-publishable study. Options: (1) Introduce a true separation: a co-reviewer or external hypothesis formalizer who does not see exploratory results until after they formalize the hypotheses (but this contradicts the 'single-operator' commitment); (2) Strengthen procedural controls: Mandate explicit hypothesis commit to protected branch BEFORE exploratory analysis phase begins, signed and timestamped, with a strict 'no-modification-after-exploration' rule (but current design defers specific hypotheses to Gate 2 by policy); (3) Reframe the contribution: Acknowledge in the methods that this is an 'exploratory-then-confirmatory' study with adaptive hypothesis selection (which is valid science, but weaker than pre-registered claims). The current design claims structural independence but implements only procedural controls. Choose one honestly.

findings.title: Code Gates Are Conditional on Phase 0 Completion — Not Yet Operational
findings.where: ADR-0015 §1, Gates architecture, Phase 0 prerequisites, implementation status
findings.problem: ADR-0015 §1 explicitly states: 'All claims that the firewall between phases is structural, not procedural — in ADR-0004, ADR-0007, README, and the Charter — are conditional on Phase 0 implementation completing before any data acquisition.' The charter claims that 'Code gates — Gate 2 is deterministic code that cannot be bypassed' provide structural protection. However, Phase 0 is not complete. None of the Phase 1 analytical agents (P1–P5) are implemented (all raise NotImplementedError). The gate topology may be structurally correct, but it is being tested only with synthetic payloads. The moment real exploratory analysis begins, the structural claims become untested. A journal reviewer would require evidence that Gate 2 actually prevented access to test data during a real exploratory run — not a synthetic test.
findings.evidence: (1) ADR-0015 hard prerequisites for structural firewall claims: 'LangGraph skeleton running with all gate nodes present, Gate 1 firing with synthetic payload confirmed, PostgresSaver checkpointing confirmed on target hardware, Gate 2 data split logic implemented and idempotency test passing, No SCF data acquired until all criteria are met'; (2) All P1–P5 agents return NotImplementedError (p1_strm_nlp/agent.py line 81, p4_risk_blindspot/agent.py, etc.); (3) data_acquisition/agent.py, embedding_agent/agent.py also NotImplementedError; (4) Phase 0 is incomplete — no real data has been ingested; (5) The structural firewall claim is conditional, not absolute
findings.recommendation: Do not claim that code gates provide structural independence until Phase 0 is complete and Gate 2 has been exercised in a real run with actual exploratory analysis (not synthetic data). The claim is defensible once implemented, but pre-maturely asserting it in a methods section makes the paper vulnerable to reviewer challenge. At publication: either complete Phase 0 and demonstrate the firewall with real data, or reframe the independence claim as 'structural design intent, operational status pending Phase 0 completion' and acknowledge this as a limitation.

findings.title: Append-Only Provenance (PROV-DM) Not Operationally Integrated
findings.where: ADR-0003 (PROV-DM decision), Orchestrator implementation, gate_coordinator_node, actual PROV-O generation
findings.problem: ADR-0003 commits to W3C PROV-DM as the canonical provenance vocabulary and states: 'The Orchestrator emits PROV-O triples automatically at each node completion. Contributors do not hand-author provenance records — they implement nodes that emit them.' However, no PROV-O generation code exists in src/. The checkpointer and gate_coordinator_node use plain datetime.now() and Python dicts. There is no PROV-DM triple generation, no RDF serialization, no validation that provenance is append-only. The charter relies on provenance as a critical independence mechanism: 'Append-only provenance — the W3C PROV-DM audit trail records every platform action before the next step executes. No step can be retroactively removed.' Without operational PROV-O, there is no verifiable audit trail, only unstructured checkpoint logs.
findings.evidence: (1) ADR-0003: 'The Orchestrator emits PROV-O triples automatically at each node completion'; (2) No grep hits for 'rdflib', 'PROV', 'ActedOnBehalfOf', or RDF serialization in src/; (3) gate_coordinator_node (lines 28–47) uses datetime.isoformat() and Python dicts; (4) checkpointer.py is PostgreSQL schema management, not provenance generation; (5) ADR-0003 states 'rdflib is mature and well-maintained' but it is not used; (6) No SPARQL query examples or RDF validation in RUNBOOK.md
findings.recommendation: Either (a) implement PROV-DM triple generation at each node completion (integrate rdflib, emit PROV-O to a local Fuseki store or file-based RDF, validate append-only semantics), or (b) demote PROV-DM to 'future archival format' and acknowledge that the current system relies on PostgreSQL checkpoint logs and git history as provenance, not W3C standard representations. Option (a) is stronger for a published study. Option (b) is honest about current capability. The ADR commits to (a); the implementation is incomplete.

findings.title: Single-Operator Independence Model Lacks Critical Review Mechanism
findings.where: Charter §8.5, Project Governance, Escalation loop (ADR-0014), gate decisions
findings.problem: The charter acknowledges the single-operator limitation and proposes that independence is achieved through 'structural transparency' instead of co-review. It lists four mechanisms: code gates, append-only provenance, timestamped pre-registration, and complete reproducibility. However, none of these prevent a biased researcher from (1) writing bad exploratory EDA summaries that lead to biased hypothesis formulation, (2) approving their own Gate 2 hypotheses without independent scrutiny, (3) selecting a primary embedding model that happens to align with their priors. The Escalation loop (ADR-0014) is designed for scope questions ('can I use new data source?'), not for research integrity checks ('is this hypothesis biased?'). Thomas would file his own escalation, Claude would help him think through it, and he would approve his own proposal. This is procedurally transparent but not independent. A journal would ask: 'Who reviewed the hypothesis set for HARKing?' The answer 'the researcher, with an LLM assistant' does not meet publication standards.
findings.evidence: (1) Charter §8.5: 'There is no independent co-reviewer' and independence comes from 'structural transparency, not co-reviewer availability'; (2) Escalation loop (ADR-0014) routes to 'the operator via Claude' — the operator is Thomas, so self-approval; (3) Gate 2 node (gates.py line 209) is an interrupt(), not a check against external criteria; (4) hypothesis_formalizer HARKing validation (line 121) is an internal check, no external escalation path; (5) The charter explicitly states this is a 'deliberate methodological position,' not an oversight — acknowledged but not mitigated
findings.recommendation: For publication in a top-tier journal, add a mandatory external review gate before Gate 2 approval. Options: (1) Pre-publication external review: Submit the exploratory findings and formalized hypothesis set to a domain expert or pre-registered reviewer (not Claude, a human) for bias check before Thomas approves Gate 2; (2) Registered report: Use OSF pre-registration + peer review of the SAP before data analysis, which is the gold standard for single-operator studies; (3) Adversarial collaboration: Invite an external researcher to co-analyze the confirmatory phase independently. Without one of these, the 'structural transparency' framing will not survive peer review. The transparency is excellent, but transparency without independent review is not independence.

findings.title: Model Selection Driven by Exploratory Analysis — Potential for Circular Reasoning
findings.where: SAP §10 (Model Selection Criteria), ADR-0013 (Primary Analysis), Phase 1/2 protocol, gate_2_node checklist (gates.py line 276)
findings.problem: The SAP (§10) specifies a two-phase model selection protocol: Phase 1 evaluates candidates on public benchmarks (STS-B, SICK-R) and SCF structural anchor pairs, Phase 2 evaluates the shortlist on the validation split using silhouette score and anchor consistency. The problem: Phase 2 uses STRM anchor pairs ('= Equal To' and '∅ No Relation') as weak supervision. STRM reliability is simultaneously under investigation in P1 (is STRM valid or not?). The model selected in Phase 2 is selected partially because it agrees with STRM anchors. Then P1 tests whether those STRM labels are valid using the selected model. If the selected model happens to agree with STRM because it was selected for agreement, the circular reasoning is baked in. SAP §12 acknowledges this: 'Anchor Consistency Circularity — This creates a partial circularity: STRM assertions are used to validate the embedding while their reliability is being tested.' The mitigation (silhouette score + cross-model agreement are STRM-independent) is weak — if all three criteria agree, it is stronger, but if silhouette is mediocre and cross-model agreement is weak, the model selection is driven by anchor consistency.
findings.evidence: (1) SAP §10 Phase 2 evaluation: 'Anchor consistency score: proportion of STRM Equal To pairs ranked above No Relation pairs'; (2) ADR-0013 specifies 'Anchor consistency score' as one of three Phase 2 criteria; (3) SAP §12 explicitly acknowledges the circularity: 'STRM assertions are used to validate the embedding while their reliability is being tested'; (4) gate_2_node checklist (gates.py, line 277): 'Primary embedding model designated from LEDGER-0002 candidates based on Phase 1 F1 scores' — but Phase 1 doesn't run until after Phase 2 model selection in current sequencing, suggesting the logic may be fuzzy
findings.recommendation: Decouple STRM anchor evaluation from model selection. Option (1): Select the primary model on silhouette + cross-model agreement only, without anchor consistency. Use anchor consistency as a secondary finding in P1 (does the selected model agree with STRM?). Option (2): If anchor consistency is required for model selection, acknowledge it explicitly in the SAP and in any findings that rely on model-STRM agreement — label those findings as potentially circular. Current design tries to have it both ways (use STRM for selection, test STRM reliability) without full transparency. For a published paper, the dependencies must be explicit.

findings.title: Hypothesis Pre-Registration Claims Are Technically Premature — Ledger Not Yet Populated
findings.where: PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md, Gate 2 timing, SAP §10 DIVERGENCE-01 decision
findings.problem: The PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md exists and is well-structured, but it has no actual entries. All three existing entries (LEDGER-0001, LEDGER-0002, LEDGER-0003) are marked 'status: pre-lock | draft' with placeholders for 'content_hash and git_commit_sha are populated at formal commit to pre-registration branch; hashes below are placeholders until that event.' The ledger promises cryptographic pre-registration, but no actual pre-registration has occurred. This is not yet a credibility problem, but it means the charter's claim of 'timestamped pre-registration — every hypothesis the platform pursues is logged' is not yet testable. A journal reviewer will request the populated ledger with git commit SHAs and RFC-3161 .tsr files. At publication, these must exist and be auditable.
findings.evidence: (1) PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md entries LEDGER-0001 through -0003 all carry 'status: pre-lock | draft'; (2) All content_hash, git_commit_sha, and rfc3161_tsr fields are marked '[to be populated at commit time]'; (3) No entries with 'status: committed' or 'status: locked' in the ledger; (4) /docs/protocol/registration/ directory does not exist; (5) SAP §10 defines DIVERGENCE-01 as 'a Gate 2 prerequisite' but 'Decision framework is defined in SAP §10. Specific operationalization is selected post-EDA, logged to Preregistration Ledger before Gate 2'
findings.recommendation: Before Gate 2 is executed for the first time: (1) Complete entries LEDGER-0001 through -0003 with actual commit SHAs and RFC-3161 timestamps (requires implementing RFC-3161 first); (2) Create protocol/registration/ directory and populate it with .tsr files; (3) Test that openssl ts -verify works on all .tsr files against the committed artifacts. At publication, include a reproducibility section that walks a reader through verifying the ledger entries. This is technically straightforward; it is currently incomplete.

findings.title: Research Contribution Reframe (P1 Primary Finding) Risks Underpowering Confirmatory Claims
findings.where: ADR-0013 (STRM Demoted), Project Charter contribution framing, P1 module design
findings.problem: The original platform goal (charter §1.5): 'The SCF claims Expert-Derived Content (EDC) produces superior crosswalk mappings versus NLP/ML approaches. No empirical validation of those 280,000+ mappings has ever been published. Every analytical module in this platform addresses some dimension of that gap.' ADR-0013 reframes this: STRM is demoted from ground truth to secondary comparison, and the primary finding becomes 'computational similarity characterization with STRM comparison.' This reframe is methodologically sound (eliminates construct contamination), but it shifts the scientific contribution from 'Is EDC superior?' (a binary claim answerable by hypothesis testing) to 'How do computational similarity and EDC differ?' (a characterization question, harder to publish as confirmatory science). The charter still lists P1 as testing H1.1–H1.6 hypotheses, but if the primary question is exploratory characterization, the confirmatory hypotheses become secondary. A journal would ask: 'What is your primary claim?' and the answer must be internally consistent.
findings.evidence: (1) Charter §1.5: 'No empirical validation of those 280,000+ mappings has ever been published. Every analytical module in this platform addresses some dimension of that gap'; (2) ADR-0013: 'Revised contribution framing: First empirical characterization of SCF semantic structure using NLP similarity methods, with comparison to expert-assigned STRM labels — including quantification of where computational similarity and expert judgment diverge'; (3) Original framing was 'validation of STRM,' new framing is 'characterization + comparison,' which are different claim classes
findings.recommendation: Choose one scientific narrative and commit to it. Option (1): Keep EDC validation as the primary question. Design P1 to test whether STRM agreement with NLP models is statistically significantly above chance / baseline. This requires independent STRM reliability estimation (Fleiss kappa), which is Path 1 in SAP §12 (rater recruitment). Option (2): Accept characterization as the primary contribution and reframe the paper as 'first computational characterization of SCF semantic space' with STRM comparison as an interesting secondary finding. Both are publishable; the current design hedges between them. For the methods section and contribution statement, be explicit about which one is primary.

result.lens: Goal-Level Architecture Sufficiency for Three-Tier Research Program

findings.title: ARA Artifact Schema Not Framework-Agnostic—Constrained to SCF-STRM Edge Type
findings.where: src/agents/base.py (STRMMapping class lines 37-69); state.py (hypothesis_set, findings structure); SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md Phase 5
findings.problem: Charter §1.5 requirement 1: 'ARA artifact schema must be framework-agnostic. The knowledge graph structure must work identically for NIST 800-53, CIS v8, and SCF.' The only edge type implemented (STRMMapping) is inherently SCF-dual: source_framework and target_framework are present, but the relationship_type enum (subset|superset|intersection|equal|no_relation) is the SCF's STRM taxonomy, not framework-neutral set theory. When Tier 2 ingests Tier 1 outputs and tries to build a cross-framework knowledge graph with edges from NIST 800-53 project + CIS v8 project + SCF project, it has no schema for typing edges that did not originate from STRM. Example: a NIST 800-53 control alignment edge discovered by community detection in the NIST project has no place in this schema—the edge_type must map to STRM's five relationships, which don't generalize. Result: Tier 2 cannot consume non-STRM framework graphs without rebuilding the schema, violating the design requirement that no schema changes are needed.
findings.evidence: STRMMapping explicitly binds relationship_type to STRM categories. The Charter (§1.5, ADR-0010 §140) states: 'The STRMMapping Pydantic model is the universal edge type — it works for any source→target framework pair.' This is true only if all edges encode STRM labels. But P2 (Control Space Topology) discovers community structure via Leiden algorithm—those edges are not STRM-labeled; they are implicit in the embedding space. The state schema (state.py) has no provision for mixed edge types.
findings.recommendation: Restructure the edge schema immediately (before exploratory analysis produces outputs that cannot be exported). Define a FrameworkAgnosticEdge base class with fields: source_control_id, target_control_id, edge_type (categorical), strength_score (numeric), source_framework_id, target_framework_id, edge_source (enum: STRM_mapping | topological_inference | convergence_clustering | risk_coverage | semantic_entanglement), confidence_score. STRMMapping becomes a subclass with edge_type constrained to STRM taxonomy. This schema works identically for Tier 1 outputs from any framework project. Without this refactor, Tier 2 ingestion breaks.

findings.title: Null Results and Positive Findings Not Identically Structured—Prevents Tier 2/3 Consumption
findings.where: src/state.py (findings list, hypothesis_set); src/agents/hypothesis_formalizer (not yet implemented); docs/protocol/03_statistical_analysis_plan.md §2 (secondary analyses template)
findings.problem: Charter §1.5 requirement 2: 'Null results are structured identically to positive findings.' Current implementation has separate fields for hypotheses and findings, but no schema for what constitutes a 'finding' entry. The state contains hypothesis_set (pre-registered hypotheses) and findings (append-only list), but the finding struct is not defined. When confirmatory analysis rejects a hypothesis, what is written to findings? The SAP (§3, §5) acknowledges findings must be documented but provides only a template, not a concrete schema. Result: When Tier 2 tries to consume Tier 1 outputs, it cannot distinguish between confirmed positive findings, null results from rejected hypotheses, and failed analyses—all three must be present in the same distribution for Tier 2's gap analysis to work. Without identical structure, Tier 2 must apply custom parsing per Tier 1 project, breaking the downstream consumption model.
findings.evidence: SAP §6 states 'Null results reported with effect size estimate and CI — no significant difference without a CI is not a finding.' But findings is typed as list[dict[str, Any]] with no schema validation. The state.py reducer for findings is a plain append (lambda a, b: a + b), meaning any dict structure is accepted. The HypothesisFormalizerAgent raises NotImplementedError (src/agents/hypothesis_formalizer/agent.py line ~20), so no actual finding struct is implemented. SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md Phase 5 says 'Each hypothesis test result has a corresponding ARA artifact entry' but does not specify the schema. Tier 2 will receive a heterogeneous list of dicts with no contract.
findings.recommendation: Define a ConfirmatoryFinding Pydantic model before any confirmatory analysis runs. Required fields: hypothesis_id (H[module].[n] format), test_name (string), test_statistic (numeric), p_value (numeric), effect_size (numeric), confidence_interval_lower (numeric), confidence_interval_upper (numeric), decision (confirmed|rejected|underpowered), interpretation (string), null_result (bool), uncertainty_sources (list[string]). Positive and null findings differ only in the decision and interpretation fields. Tier 2 consumes the full list and filters by finding type. Implement before Gate 2; log schema to Preregistration Ledger.

findings.title: Confidence Scores Not Implemented—Blocks Tier 3 ML Features
findings.where: src/agents/base.py (ControlMetadata, STRMMapping have no confidence field); state.py (eda_artifacts, prov_activities contain no confidence); SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md §250 (design requirement for Tier 3)
findings.problem: Charter §1.5 requirement 3: 'Confidence scores on all findings. Tier 3 uses these as ML model features. If findings don't carry confidence scores, the Tier 3 feature space is impoverished.' Current implementation has strength_score (1–10) on STRM mappings but no confidence score derived from empirical evidence. When P1 runs correlation analysis, the model-STRM divergence metric (DIVERGENCE-01, SAP §10) will produce a numeric score; that score is the natural confidence measure—how much do models agree that a control relationship is strong? But there is no field in the schema to carry this confidence forward through P2, P3, P4, P5 into the final findings. Result: Tier 3 receives only binary positive/null findings with no gradient of certainty. The causal forest or SHAP model in Tier 3 will have impoverished features—it cannot weight findings by confidence, cannot rank control importance by empirical certainty, and cannot propagate uncertainty through impact models.
findings.evidence: STRMMapping and ControlMetadata classes have no confidence_score field. ConfirmatoryFinding (once implemented) will have p_value and CI but not a continuous confidence score across the 0-1 range capturing model agreement, effect size coherence, or cross-framework consistency. SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md (line 250) explicitly states this as a Tier 1 design requirement: 'ARA artifact schema must include confidence scores on all findings (Tier 3 uses these as model features).' The SAP's model selection protocol (ADR-0013, §Phase 2) computes anchor_consistency_score but does not propagate it to per-finding confidence. Each P-module must output findings with confidence, but no mechanism exists.
findings.recommendation: Add a confidence_score field to all result objects: P1 findings get confidence = cross_model_agreement (Spearman pairwise correlation across embedding models, 0–1); P2 findings get confidence = community_detection_stability (rerun community detection k times, measure agreement); P3 findings get confidence = lrf_coverage_consistency (multi-LRF consensus on domain emphasis); P4 findings get confidence = coverage_distribution_entropy (inverse of standard deviation across framework portfolios, normalized); P5 findings get confidence = clustering_cohesion (Silhouette score on final clusters). All confidence scores must be included in ARA artifact exports. Document the operationalization in the SAP before Gate 2.

findings.title: P2 Topology Graphs Not Designed for Multi-Graph Composition—Blast Radius Model Cannot Execute
findings.where: src/agents/p2_control_topology/agent.py (stub, not implemented); docs/architecture/SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md Phase T2-5 (blast radius modeling); docs/decisions/ADR-0010 §63-64
findings.problem: Charter §1.5 requirement 4: 'P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition. The blast radius model in Tier 2 runs graph diffusion across the combined cross-framework topology.' The P2 agent is not yet implemented (raises NotImplementedError), so no graph schema exists. The Roadmap (Phase T2-5) specifies 'Information cascade / graph diffusion; simulate control failure propagation across the cross-framework topology.' But propagation across multiple framework graphs requires edges to be tagged with their source framework so Tier 2 can treat same-control-ID nodes in different frameworks as distinct (NIST-800-53 AC-1 ≠ SCF AC-1, but both may exist in the cross-framework topology). The current schema has framework as an optional metadata field on controls but does not specify how edges compose across framework boundaries. Without this, diffusion algorithms cannot execute: a failure in SCF AC-1 will incorrectly propagate to NIST AC-1 as if they were the same node.
findings.evidence: P2ControlTopologyAgent.run_exploratory() raises NotImplementedError (src/agents/p2_control_topology/agent.py). The agent is PROTECTED (prompting changes require escalation), indicating its analysis is load-bearing, but the implementation does not exist. The SWARM Roadmap specifies that P2 will use 'NetworkX, PyG, iGraph' and 'Leiden, Louvain, Girvan-Newman' (docs/charter/PROJECT_CHARTER.md §299-305) but the LangGraph state has no graph-typed field—all analysis outputs are appended to findings/eda_artifacts as files, not structures that Tier 2 can algorithmically consume. When Tier 2's 'Phase T2-5: Blast Radius Modeling' reads P2 outputs from all framework projects, it will receive Parquet tables or JSON files—not composable graph objects with unified node IDs, framework tags, and cross-framework edge semantics.
findings.recommendation: Before P2 implementation, design a ComposableGraph Pydantic model: nodes=[{node_id, framework_id, control_id, domain, metadata}], edges=[{source_node_id, target_node_id, edge_type, strength, source_framework_id, source_module (P1|P2|P3|P4|P5), timestamp}]. All node IDs must be globally unique across frameworks (format: framework_id:control_id). P2 outputs its discovered topology as a ComposableGraph serialized to Parquet. Tier 2 ingests multiple ComposableGraphs, merges them with node ID deduplication, and runs diffusion algorithms on the combined structure. Document the schema in a new ARA_GRAPH_FORMAT.md before P2 implementation.

findings.title: No Tier-1-to-Tier-2 Data Handoff Protocol—Operational Continuity Undefined
findings.where: docs/architecture/SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md (Phase T2-0 to T2-1 boundary); docs/decisions/ADR-0010 (no handoff specification); src/agents/data_acquisition/ (DVC integration not implemented)
findings.problem: The charter and ADRs specify that Tier 1 exports 'ARA artifacts: structured JSON/Parquet knowledge graphs for Tier 2 consumption' (ADR-0010 §40) and Tier 2 'Inputs are the exported knowledge graphs from each Tier 1 project (JSON/Parquet format, consumed via DVC remote)' (SWARM Roadmap §212). But there is no specification of: (1) what constitutes a complete ARA export, (2) how DVC remotes are configured across projects, (3) what validation Tier 2 runs before ingestion, (4) how metadata (framework version, analysis timestamp, pre-registration link) is preserved in the export, (5) what happens if Tier 1 discovers a SAP violation and invalidates its results—does Tier 2 re-run downstream? The DataAcquisitionAgent's run() method is not implemented, so the export pipeline does not exist. Result: When Tier 1 completes, there is no operational path to move results into Tier 2.
findings.evidence: SWARM Roadmap Phase 5 (line 134) says 'ARA deliverables in ara/ are complete and externally consumable' but ara/ does not exist (find search returned no ara/ directory in the project). DataAcquisitionAgent documentation says 'Run ARA Compiler — convert XLSX to JSON+Parquet in ara/artifacts/' (src/agents/data_acquisition/agent.py line 95) but ARA Compiler is not defined anywhere in the codebase. The Roadmap Phase T2-0 says 'DVC remote configured to receive Tier 1 ARA artifacts' but no DVC configuration exists, and no cross-project DVC setup documentation is written.
findings.recommendation: Before Tier 1 completion, create ARA_EXPORT_SPEC.md defining: (1) required artifacts (all hypothesis results, all findings, all null results, P2 graph, P3 convergence matrix, P4 coverage maps, P5 clusters, PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER, manuscript draft); (2) file structure and naming (e.g., findings_T1_SCF_2026_06_15.parquet, graph_T1_SCF_2026_06_15.parquet); (3) metadata schema (framework_name, framework_version, tier, analysis_date, pre_registration_commit, gate_2_approval_date); (4) validation checksum (SHA256 of all artifacts, signed with project key); (5) DVC directory structure for cross-project consumption (projects/hc-grc-tier-1-scf/artifacts/, projects/hc-grc-tier-1-nist-800-53/artifacts/, etc.). Implement the export pipeline in DataAcquisitionAgent before Phase 5 completes.

findings.title: Research Methodology Treats Exploratory Hypothesis Formation as Unbiased, But Protocol Lacks Outcome-Independence Mechanism
findings.where: docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md §3 (exploratory hypothesis formation without independence check); docs/protocol/03_statistical_analysis_plan.md §12.Exploratory-Guided Hypothesis Selection; ADR-0013 (no validation that exploratory hypotheses pre-specify effect sizes from theory, not observed data)
findings.problem: The SAP (§12) explicitly acknowledges that 'Hypotheses entering Gate 2 are selected based on what looked tractable or promising in the exploratory phase' and that this introduces 'effect size inflation relative to theory-derived hypotheses.' The mitigation is 'Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction applied across all confirmatory hypotheses regardless of whether they are theory-derived or data-adaptive.' But the protocol does not implement the necessary upstream control: it does not require that exploratory analysis is conducted in a way that produces hypotheses with pre-specified effect sizes from theory, not from observed data. The HypothesisFormalizerAgent (not yet implemented) will receive exploratory findings and generate hypotheses, but there is no check that the effect sizes it proposes come from literature or theory, not from observed effect sizes in the exploratory data (which would be double-dipping). Result: The correction method (Benjamini-Hochberg) is necessary but insufficient—it corrects for multiple testing, not for data-driven effect size inflation.
findings.evidence: Methods Scaffolding §2.1 specifies 'Minimum effect size worth reporting' and §2.2 specifies 'Pre-specified success criteria' but then §2.4 (Power Analysis) says 'Effect size estimates from literature or pilot data' with no mechanism to distinguish them. The SAP (§3) does not define how effect sizes are obtained. ADR-0013 specifies that primary model selection will be 'theory-derived' vs. 'data-adaptive' but the P1 model evaluation (Phase 1 benchmark on STS-B, SICK-R; Phase 2 calibration on validation split) produces evidence that informs effect size estimates—there is a structural risk that exploratory findings will be incorporated into post-hoc effect size thresholds. No procedural safeguard exists in the code or protocol to prevent this.
findings.recommendation: Add to the SAP before Gate 2: a table of all pre-specified effect size thresholds with their sources (literature reference, theory, or 'pre-specified from exploratory calibration on validation split'). For data-adaptive effect sizes, require an explicit justification that the effect size is not merely the observed effect size in the exploratory sample (which would inflate power estimates). The HypothesisFormalizerAgent must document the source of each effect size before the hypothesis is locked. Implement a validation check in Gate 2 that rejects any hypothesis with effect_size_source='observed_exploratory' unless it comes with a separate calibration sample (not the validation split used to select the model).

findings.title: State Schema Lacks Explicit Representation of Multi-Module Dependencies and Cross-Module Null Results
findings.where: src/state.py (eda_artifacts append-only; eda_agent_statuses simple list; no cross-module dependency graph); src/nodes/phase1.py (exploratory_phase_node executes P1–P5 sequentially with no module-to-module data flow)
findings.problem: The charter states that P1–P5 'are not sequential — they are parallel research tracks that share infrastructure, data models, and findings. Module outputs feed each other.' (PROJECT_CHARTER.md §2). But the implementation executes them sequentially (exploratory_phase_node, line ~14 comment: 'P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5 (sequential; TODO: Send() fan-out)'). More critically, there is no schema representation of module dependencies—P4 (Risk Blindspot) depends on P1 (STRM calibration quality) to know which mappings are reliable, and P5 (AI Governance Clustering) depends on P2 (topology) to understand which domains it cuts across. But eda_artifacts is an append-only list of file paths with no metadata about which module produced which, whether a module's output is a prerequisite for another, or what to do if a module fails. If P1 fails to produce reliable embeddings, P5 (which depends on embeddings for cross-domain clustering) should receive a signal—but the state schema does not carry this dependency.
findings.evidence: state.py eda_agent_statuses is a list of {agent_id, status, note, timestamp} dicts with no dependency information. If P1 fails, P5 is still invoked (the exploratory_phase_node does not short-circuit). SWARM Roadmap Phase 3 says modules run 'in parallel' and lists them as independent in the table (lines 89-95), but the implementation is sequential. Phase 3 success criteria say 'A P3 failure produces a null result entry, does not cancel P1/P2/P4/P5' (line 106), which assumes independence—but P4 and P5 are not independent of P1's embedding quality.
findings.recommendation: Add to HCGRCState: a module_dependency_graph field (dict[module_id, list[upstream_modules]]) specifying P5 depends on P2, P4 depends on P1, etc. Implement a pre-module health check in exploratory_phase_node that evaluates upstream module readiness before invoking a module. If an upstream module's status is 'failed', the dependent module receives a degraded_upstream signal and either adjusts its analysis (e.g., P5 uses a fallback clustering approach) or produces a conditional null result: 'Cannot execute due to upstream dependency failure.' Document this dependency graph in P1–P5 AGENT.md cards.

findings.title: Confirmatory Analysis Lacks Explicit Specification of Secondary and Sensitivity Analysis Plan
findings.where: docs/protocol/03_statistical_analysis_plan.md §2 (secondary analyses template with empty rows); §3 (sensitivity analyses template with empty rows); docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md §4 (hyperparameter handling defers 'specific test variant choices')
findings.problem: The SAP is templated but not populated. The critical section for Tier 2 robustness is the specification of sensitivity analyses—results that hold under alternative analytical choices (embedding model, clustering parameters, community detection algorithm, divergence metric operationalization). Tier 2 needs to know which results are robust and which are sensitive to methodological choices. But the SAP §3 is empty except for headers. The HypothesisFormalizerAgent will populate this before Gate 2, but the protocol does not constrain how many sensitivity analyses are required, how they relate to primary findings, or how Tier 2 should interpret them. Result: When Tier 2 receives Tier 1 outputs, it cannot distinguish between robust and fragile findings without re-running sensitivity analyses itself—duplicating effort and introducing new opportunities for data contamination.
findings.evidence: SAP §2 and §3 have placeholder rows with no entries. Methods Scaffolding §3 specifies 'all analyses must pre-specify correction method in the SAP. The correction method is not chosen after seeing uncorrected p-values' (line 135) but then §4 says 'Hyperparameters that affect the structure of the analysis must be pre-specified' while also deferring 'specific test variant choices within the families named above' (line 189). No procedural gate exists to ensure sensitivity analyses are locked before confirmatory testing begins.
findings.recommendation: Create a SENSITIVITY_ANALYSIS_SPEC.md template before Gate 2 defining: for each primary hypothesis, what are the three most critical analytical choices (model selection, parameter tuning, statistical test variant), and for each choice, what are the plausible alternatives? For P1 (embedding model), sensitivity analyses must test the full candidate model set. For P2 (community detection), must test Leiden, Louvain, and Girvan-Newman at different resolution parameters. For P3–P5, specify analogous alternatives. Require HypothesisFormalizerAgent to populate this template and lock it at Gate 2 alongside primary hypotheses. Include sensitivity results in ARA exports so Tier 2 can assess robustness.

findings.title: Exploratory-Confirmatory Firewall Cannot Detect Mid-Tier Scope Creep or Hypothesis Post-Hoc Specification
findings.where: docs/protocol/02_contribution.md (incomplete, no scope boundary definition); docs/protocol/00_research_questions.md §Scope Boundary (does not address mid-tier drift); ADR-0012 §Escalation (gates scope, but not hypothesis selection drift)
findings.problem: The firewall prevents confirmatory analysis before exploratory characterization is complete and prevents accessing the test split before Gate 2 approval. But it does not prevent hypothesis drift within the exploratory phase—if the HypothesisFormalizerAgent observes that P1 embeddings cluster by domain, it could propose a hypothesis 'editorial domains correspond to semantic communities' that was not in the exploratory research questions. This is not a Gate 2 violation (it's still pre-registered before confirmatory testing), but it violates the scientific principle of hypothesis independence from the data that will test it. The protocol acknowledges this risk (SAP §12) but mitigates it only post-hoc via Benjamini-Hochberg—it does not prevent it upfront.
findings.evidence: contribution.md §What This Project Does NOT Claim is incomplete ('[To be populated]'). research_questions.md §Scope Boundary lists out-of-scope questions (comparison to non-SCF frameworks, prescriptive guidance) but does not establish a boundary for hypothesis scope—which prevents the Escalation routine from detecting when exploratory findings suggest a new research question that was not part of the original five (P1–P5).
findings.recommendation: Complete contribution.md with a scope boundary section: 'Hypotheses must address only the five core research questions (P1–P5). Hypotheses about framework comparison, organizational impact, or control failure causation are out of scope for Tier 1 and must trigger Escalation via ADR-0014.' Implement a semantic check in Gate 2 that parses hypothesis text and flags any hypothesis that references concepts not in the original five RQs (e.g., 'causal inference', 'financial impact', 'organizational', 'industry-specific'). Require escalation approval before such hypotheses can be locked.

result.lens: Architecture and Framework Mismatch Analysis: Goal Achievement Assessment

findings.title: LLM-as-Orchestrator Fundamental Mismatch: Reasoning-Heavy Architecture for Deterministic Numerical Research
findings.where: src/reasoning_client.py, ADR-0016, entire agent orchestration architecture
findings.problem: The platform routes ~70% of computational work (embeddings, graph algorithms, statistical testing, clustering, coverage analysis) through a tiered LLM reasoning seam (Tiers 1-3, with Tier 2/3 inference) despite these being deterministic numerical operations. ADR-0016 claims Tier 1 is 'deterministic ML, no LLM' but reasoning_client.complete() is the abstraction boundary, and the code structure assumes agents call complete() or reasoning-based decisions flow through Ollama/Claude-Max. The fundamental tension: a research platform conducting large-scale empirical ML should isolate inference to high-stakes judgment moments (hypothesis formalization, inter-rater reliability assessment, interpretation). Instead, the architecture invites agency-through-inference into P1-P5 exploratory and confirmatory analysis. This couples scientific rigor to LLM output quality on tasks where deterministic computation is both possible and preferable.
findings.evidence: ADR-0016 defines Tier 2 as 'low-impact, high-volume reasoning served by locally hosted open model' including 'P1 per-pair STRM classification at scale.' P1 agent card states all 5 NLP method tiers must run (lines 21-27 of agent.py), but classification is delegated to T2/T3. The reasoning_client has 300+ lines of hardening for timeout/retry/backpressure/saturation handling that should not be necessary for deterministic statistical operations. The GOAL requires embedding 1,400 controls × 200+ LRF sets across embedding models and computing similarity matrices at scale—this is matrix computation, not reasoning. Routing through Ollama introduces non-determinism (greedy decoding, temperature sampling, retry variability) into what should be bit-reproducible numerical analysis.
findings.recommendation: Separate Tier 1 deterministic work into direct scikit-learn/PyG/scipy calls that bypass reasoning_client entirely. Reserve Tier 2 for interactive triage/classification where human-in-the-loop or heuristic judgment is required (edge-case categorization). Reserve Tier 3 (Claude-Max) only for gate decisions, genuine ambiguity resolution, and final scientific claims. Introduce a `src/compute/` layer that contains all P1-P5 numerical pipelines. This preserves the reasoning_client seam for authentic reasoning work while avoiding the architectural coupling that forces embedding/clustering/testing through LLM backends.

findings.title: GPU/Compute Requirement Unaddressed: Mac mini M-series CPU-only Embedding and Graph Algorithms at Scale
findings.where: Platform.yaml, src/agents/embedding_agent/agent.py, PROJECT_CHARTER.md §9 open questions, docs/decisions/ADR-0016
findings.problem: The GOAL explicitly flags 'GPU need; the node is a Mac mini' in the prompt (open question #4 in charter). The actual implementation in embedding_agent.py specifies 'Batch-encode curated control text (GPU if available, CPU fallback)' but no GPU compute is configured, no CUDA/Metal Performance Shaders acceleration is set up, and no hardware benchmarking has been done to establish whether a Mac mini can complete the required compute within acceptable time. The charter specifies 5 embedding model candidates (all-MiniLM, BGE, E5, legal-BERT, SPLADE) must be evaluated in Phase 1, then P1 runs all NLP method tiers (TF-IDF, BM25, 5 dense models, LDA/BERTopic, NLI entailment, hybrid ensemble) on ~280K control-pair vectors. The architecture assumes local Ollama (T2) runs on-device for bulk reasoning, yet Ollama with llama3.1:8b is primarily a CPU inference engine—allocating Mac mini CPU to both embedding/graph compute AND local LLM inference creates resource contention that is unquantified.
findings.evidence: Platform.yaml does not specify GPU device or Metal acceleration flags. EmbeddingAgent notes 'GPU if available, CPU fallback' but provides no initialization code for torch.device or mps device configuration. SAP §3 defines 'Phase 1 benchmark evaluation' on STS-B, SICK-R, and regulatory benchmarks as prerequisites (LEDGER-0002 candidate pre-registration), requiring N forward passes through each model on 1,400+ controls before Gate 2 model selection. This alone is tens of billions of multiply-accumulate operations. P2 graph algorithms (Leiden, Louvain, centrality metrics) on 280K edges at scale; P5 HDBSCAN on 1,400+ embeddings in UMAP-reduced space; P1 all-pairs similarity requires ~280K × 280K × embedding_dim (typically 384–1024) floating-point operations. The charter explicitly raises GPU need as an open question and defers to 'production runs.' No Phase 0 hardware feasibility study has been executed. The platform is live on a node without GPU access and no timeline for relocating to GPU infrastructure is documented.
findings.recommendation: Establish hardware feasibility before Phase 1 data acquisition: (1) Run Phase 0 benchmarks with actual embedding model candidates (all-MiniLM, BGE-base) on 1,400 synthetic controls, measure wall-clock time and peak memory on Mac mini. (2) If execution time exceeds 24 hours for Phase 1 embedding generation, escalate to operator for cloud GPU compute decision (Lambda Labs, Vast.ai referenced in ADR-0016 but never operationalized). (3) Implement device selection in src/infrastructure/compute.py: detect GPU (CUDA/MPS), fall back to CPU, log hardware profile to experiment metadata. (4) Update platform.yaml with optional 'compute.device' config and document fallback behavior. The current architecture has GPU as a 'nice-to-have' with silent CPU fallback; it should be a precondition gate.

findings.title: DVC Data Versioning Declared but Not Wired: Embedding Manifests and Splits Referencing DVC Hashes Without Integration
findings.where: src/agents/embedding_agent/agent.py, src/agents/data_steward/agent.py, requirements.txt (dvc>=3.0), src/agents/data_acquisition/agent.py
findings.problem: The GOAL requires reproducible artifact versioning with DVC (Data Version Control) as the mechanism for provenance tracking per the charter. EmbeddingAgent stores 'corpus_dvc_hash' and 'faiss_index_dvc_hash' in the manifest, DataStewardAgent stores 'split_dvc_hash,' and DataAcquisitionAgent declares 'Required tools: mcp-dvc' (agent.py line 18). However, no DVC initialization code exists in the src/infrastructure or agent implementations, no `.dvc/config` file is set up with a remote, and no `dvc push/pull` operations are implemented anywhere in src/. The manifest fields are future-facing (phase 1 stub: NotImplementedError), but the architectural assumption that 'embedding_manifest.faiss_index_dvc_hash' will be correctly populated and tracked is not supported by current infrastructure. This breaks the reproducibility contract: without DVC integration, the SAP requirement 'All data versioning: DVC — local remote only' (Phase 5.3) cannot be satisfied. Gate 2 approval depends on confirming 'which model produced the embeddings used in exploratory analysis' via embedding_manifest, but if DVC is not wired, there is no cryptographic link back to the exact data versions and model checksums used.
findings.evidence: dvc>=3.0,<4 is pinned in requirements.txt. The .dvc/ directory exists but .dvc/config is not versioned (gitignored). EmbeddingAgent raises NotImplementedError and states 'DVC push FAISS index to local remote' as step 8 of Phase 1 (line 118), but no DVC client imports or calls exist in the agent code. DataStewardAgent docstring references 'split_dvc_hash: str = "" # DVC hash covering all three splits' (agents/data_steward) but the actual computation is unimplemented. No MCP tools (mcp-dvc) are registered in the graph, and no instructions exist for configuring the local DVC remote on a Mac mini (should be `dvc remote add -d local /path/to/remote`). The RESEARCH_DESIGN.md standard (Phase 5.3) requires exact version pinning and reproducibility packages, but the data pipeline has declared DVC as the mechanism without implementing the integration.
findings.recommendation: Implement src/infrastructure/dvc_setup.py: (1) Initialize local DVC remote on the Mac mini at a designated path (e.g., ~/hc-grc-dvc-remote, documented in RUNBOOK.md). (2) Add dvc_config validation to config.py alongside Loopback validation: ensure .dvc/config specifies a local remote. (3) In DataAcquisitionAgent and DataStewardAgent, after data files are written, call `dvc add <file>` and `dvc push` to compute and store the hash. (4) In EmbeddingAgent, compute FAISS index hash via dvc add and record to manifest before releasing. (5) Modify SAP Gate 2 artifacts to include .dvc files as proof of version sealing. (6) Update RUNBOOK.md with DVC remote setup and troubleshooting. Without this, the reproducibility guarantee that SoPs and pre-registration depend on is aspirational.

findings.title: Qdrant Vector Store Declared but No Schema Enforcement or Semantic Search Integration in P1-P5
findings.where: src/infrastructure/vector_store/qdrant_setup.py, EmbeddingAgent, platform.yaml, P1-P5 agents
findings.problem: Platform.yaml declares Qdrant collections: hcgrc_controls, hcgrc_mappings, hcgrc_literature, hcgrc_findings (line 172–177). qdrant_setup.py provides client factory and health check but no schema definition, metadata field design, or insertion pipeline. EmbeddingAgent raises NotImplementedError and promises to 'Build Qdrant collection: hcgrc_controls (1,400+ controls)' and 'hcgrc_mappings (280K+ STRM pair embeddings)' but no code exists to: (1) structure payloads with SCF control metadata (domain, maturity, risk categories), (2) configure Qdrant's metadata filtering to enable 'query controls in domain X with MCR/DSR flag Y', (3) actually insert embeddings, (4) validate collection creation against expected schema. P1-P5 agents never import qdrant_client, never perform semantic search, never leverage the supposedly 'complementary' Qdrant infrastructure. The purpose of Qdrant (per the charter: 'semantic search, all agents') is not operationalized. This is a half-built infrastructure layer that creates a Docker dependency without delivering analytical value.
findings.evidence: qdrant_setup.py has ~80 lines defining connection helpers and health check. No collection schema definition, no metadata index configuration, no insertion workflow. EmbeddingAgent.run() raises NotImplementedError without sketching how embeddings are loaded into Qdrant. P1 agent (agent.py) specifies all-pairs STRM similarity computation but never mentions querying Qdrant; it assumes FAISS index only. P2 agent promises to 'write EXP_P2_graph.graphml' but has no Qdrant schema for control nodes. P3, P4, P5 never reference Qdrant. The infrastructure/vector_store/__init__.py contains only imports, no service initialization. The declared purpose ('semantic search, all agents') is aspiration; the actual code path is Pandas + FAISS + NetworkX.
findings.recommendation: Either (1) commit to Qdrant by designing metadata schema (control_id, domain, mcr_dsr, risk_categories as filterable fields), implementing insertion in EmbeddingAgent, and adding semantic search methods to P2-P5 for cross-domain control lookup—or (2) remove Qdrant from platform.yaml, tear down docker-compose Qdrant service, and rely on FAISS + Pandas for P1-P5 analysis, with Qdrant reserved for Tier 2 future work. Current state: declared but unutilized infrastructure, Docker dependency, maintenance burden with no analytical payoff. If the GOAL requires semantic search for efficiency (querying similar controls by embedding, filtering by domain), Qdrant design should be locked before Phase 1; if P1-P5 can be served by local FAISS, remove the infrastructure to reduce complexity.

findings.title: Exploratory/Confirmatory Firewall Implementation Incomplete: Gate 2 Data Split Integrity Not Cryptographically Sealed
findings.where: src/nodes/data_split.py, ADR-0015 Decision 5 (Data Split Idempotency), SAP §1.5 (test set access control), GATES.md
findings.problem: The GOAL's hard requirement is 'strict exploratory/confirmatory firewall: exploration on train+val, confirmation on held-out TEST split, the two phases share no data.' ADR-0015 Decision 5 specifies the test split must be deterministically seeded from manifest SHA-256 and that 'Run compute_data_split() twice with identical state. Assert bit-identical output. This test must pass before Gate 2 is considered operational.' However, the actual implementation does not seal the split irreversibly: SAP §1.5 states 'The test split is physically inaccessible before Gate 2 (Orchestrator-enforced, LangGraph topology)' but the enforcement is documented as 'encrypted DVC artifact at split time. The decryption key is held by the Orchestrator and released only at Gate 2.' This is not cryptographic sealing—it is a convention (LangGraph routing) that can be bypassed if someone modifies the graph topology or the Orchestrator node. The idempotency test is listed as a Phase 0 success criterion (ADR-0015 Decision 1) but is not implemented in Phase 0. Phase 0 currently runs with synthetic_control_ids (graph.py line 94), not with the real manifest. Without cryptographic sealing (e.g., signed/timestamped split manifest), the firewall claim is aspirational.
findings.evidence: src/nodes/data_split.py raises NotImplementedError for actual split logic. The data_split_node in Phase 0 wraps synthetic IDs (graph.py, lines 93–96). The test set is stored in encrypted form per state documentation ('The decryption key is held by the Orchestrator') but no encryption implementation exists. SAP §1.5 claims 'physical inaccessibility before Gate 2 (Orchestrator-enforced, LangGraph topology)' but physical accessibility is enforced only by graph routing and state key access patterns—neither is cryptographic. An adversarial actor modifying HCGRCState.test_ids directly or adding an exploratory node that accesses test data after Gate 2 approval (but before confirmatory run) would not be caught. The Phase 0 success criteria (ADR-0015) list 'Gate 2 data split logic implemented and idempotency test passing' but the code does not contain an idempotency test. There is no assertion that compute_data_split() twice produces bit-identical splits.
findings.recommendation: Implement Phase 0 hard gate: (1) In src/nodes/data_split.py, implement compute_data_split(manifest_hash: str) that derives seed deterministically and returns bit-identical splits on repeated calls (write idempotency test in tests/test_data_split.py). (2) After split computation, sign the split manifest (control_ids per split) using RFC 3161 timestamp + SHA-256 to create an immutable seal. (3) Store the signed manifest with timestamp in encrypted form (use cryptography.fernet or similar; key held by Orchestrator). (4) Gate 2 can only proceed if the test split has been previously sealed and the seal is valid. (5) Any attempt to modify test_ids in state after Gate 2 approval should cryptographically fail on signature verification. (6) Document the sealing procedure in GATES.md and RUNBOOK.md. This converts 'the firewall is structural, enforced by LangGraph routing' from an operational claim into a cryptographic guarantee.

findings.title: 46 NotImplementedError Stubs in Phase 1 Agents: Exploratory Analysis Cannot Execute, Hypothesis Formation Is Unimplemented
findings.where: src/agents/ (all P1-P5, hypothesis_formalizer, statistical_analyst agents), src/nodes/phase1.py, src/agents/base.py
findings.problem: The entire exploratory analysis phase (Phase 1) that feeds Gate 2 hypothesis formalization is unimplemented. All agent run() and run_exploratory() methods raise NotImplementedError. This is not a problem in principle—Phase 0 is about framework verification—but it means the critical path to executing the GOAL has never been stepped through: (1) The EmbeddingAgent does not actually embed controls. (2) P1 does not compare NLP similarity to STRM labels. (3) P2 does not detect communities or compute centrality. (4) P3 does not analyze cross-framework convergence. (5) P4 does not identify risk coverage gaps. (6) P5 does not cluster AI governance controls. (7) HypothesisFormalizerAgent does not formalize hypotheses from exploratory findings. (8) The graph architecture (data pipeline → exploratory analysis → hypothesis formalization → Gate 2) has never been validated with real analysis work. The architecture's assumption that agents can compose findings into hypotheses that then feed a SAP lock is untested. The SAP assumes exploratory output will 'generate hypotheses' (Phase 2 deferred finalization), but the mechanism for turning P1-P5 findings into formalized hypotheses H1.1–H5.4 does not exist.
findings.evidence: grep -r 'NotImplementedError' in src/agents/ yields 46 instances. Every P1-P5 agent and the hypothesis_formalizer carry Phase 1 stubs. phase1.py contains elaborate error handling (try/except NotImplementedError, stub_pending status recording) that acknowledges stubs are expected but accommodates them in tests. The architectural design assumes the full exploratory pipeline runs, but the code path cannot be executed. The RESEARCH_DESIGN.md document (Section 4.1) specifies that 'Model evaluation, Error analysis, Robustness checks, Interpretability' all happen as part of Phase 4 evaluation. But Phase 1 exploratory is supposed to generate the hypotheses for Phase 2 confirmatory testing. Without Phase 1 implementation, the hypothesis set is phantom—the SAP cannot be meaningfully finalized at Gate 2 because there is no exploratory data to ground the hypotheses in.
findings.recommendation: This is not a code review issue; it is a scope/timeline issue. Phase 0 was intended to be a dry-run verification of orchestration, gates, and state management with synthetic data. Phase 1 implementation—the actual exploratory analysis—must be scoped, scheduled, and resourced separately. The current code state is valid for Phase 0 (gates, checkpointing, data split logic, reasoning_client can all be tested without analysis agents implemented). To execute the GOAL, Phase 1 must be funded and implemented with explicit resource allocation per module (P1 NLP, P2 graph, P3 convergence analysis, P4 coverage, P5 clustering). Each agent requires: (1) data I/O from splits, (2) analysis algorithm implementation, (3) EXP_ output writing, (4) artifact composition for Gate 3 review. Estimate: 8–12 weeks of focused development per module with ML engineer + GRC domain expert collaboration.

findings.title: Multi-Model Evaluation Pipeline (LEDGER-0002) Declared but Not Operationalized: No Framework for Model Selection at Scale
findings.where: platform.yaml (model_candidates), ADR-0013 (Phase 1/2 model evaluation protocol), SAP §10, EmbeddingAgent, P1 agent
findings.problem: The GOAL requires 'embedding 1,400+ controls × 200+ LRF sets across dozens of models' (prompt emphasis). ADR-0013 specifies a two-phase model evaluation: Phase 1 on published STS benchmarks (STS-B, SICK-R) plus regulatory benchmarks (LexGLUE, ObliQA), then Phase 2 on the validation split using silhouette score + anchor consistency + cross-model agreement. Five model candidates are pre-registered in platform.yaml (all-MiniLM, BGE-base, E5-base, legal-BERT, SPLADE). However, the framework for running this evaluation does not exist in code: (1) No benchmark dataset loaders (STS-B, SICK-R, LexGLUE, ObliQA) are implemented. (2) No evaluation harness computes Spearman correlation on benchmark pairs. (3) No anchor pair selection logic (STRM '= Equal To' vs '∅ No Relation') for Phase 2 calibration. (4) No model-loading abstraction that handles diverse architectures (dense transformers, legal-BERT, SPLADE sparse). (5) No composite scoring mechanism (equal weight? optimized weights?) to select the primary model at Gate 2. The SAP promises model selection is 'locked to Preregistration Ledger with cryptographic timestamp before Gate 2 confirmatory split executes' but the decision is made with unimplemented machinery.
findings.evidence: platform.yaml line 44: 'primary_model: null # set at Gate 2 — DO NOT populate before Gate 2.' Lines 48–87 list five model candidates with roles (general_purpose, diversity_anchor_legal, diversity_anchor_sparse) but no code handles this schema. SAP §10 specifies Phase 1 evaluation on STS-B, SICK-R, and regulatory benchmarks, but no implementation loads or scores these datasets. phase1_benchmarks section of platform.yaml names them (LexGLUE, ObliQA) but the actual dataset download and evaluation is unimplemented. EmbeddingAgent Phase 1 sketch mentions 'All 5 candidates (3 general + 2 diversity anchors)' must be evaluated but the implementation is NotImplementedError. No code path exists that (a) loads 5 models, (b) embeds STS-B/SICK-R/regulatory benchmark pairs, (c) computes F1/Spearman/MRR metrics, (d) selects shortlist, (e) evaluates on validation split.
findings.recommendation: Implement src/infrastructure/model_selection.py with the full Phase 1/2 protocol: (1) ModelCandidate dataclass with fields from platform.yaml (id, label, role, local_cache). (2) BenchmarkEvaluator class that loads STS-B/SICK-R/regulatory datasets, encodes them with each candidate, computes Spearman/F1/MRR. (3) AnchorPairSelector that loads SCF splits, extracts STRM '=' pairs (similarity anchors) and '∅' pairs (dissimilarity anchors) stratified by domain. (4) ValidationEvaluator that computes silhouette score, anchor consistency, cross-model agreement on validation split. (5) ModelSelector that combines Phase 1 and Phase 2 scores, logs to Preregistration Ledger with timestamp. (6) Integration in EmbeddingAgent: load candidates from platform.yaml, run evaluators, designate primary_model, update manifest. The Phase 1/2 protocol is scientifically sound (ADR-0013); the code to execute it must be built before exploratory analysis can proceed.

findings.title: Pre-Registration Ledger and PROV-DM Infrastructure Designed but Not Integrated: Audit Trail Cannot Be Sealed at Execution Time
findings.where: docs/protocol/PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md, src/state.py (prov_activities field), src/agents/, SAP §1.3 (RFC 3161 timestamps)
findings.problem: The GOAL mandates 'pre-registration with single-operator structural independence (code gates, append-only PROV-DM, RFC-3161 timestamps).' HCGRCState includes a prov_activities field (append-only list) and the charter references 'W3C PROV-DM serialization' with JSON-LD output. However, no infrastructure exists to: (1) Generate RFC 3161 timestamps (requires a TSA endpoint or local TSA emulation; none is configured). (2) Serialize PROV-DM records (rdflib is in requirements.txt but never imported; no serialization code). (3) Seal the preregistration ledger (PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md is a template with '[To be populated]' placeholders; no code appends to it at hypothesis registration time). (4) Verify the audit trail (no mechanism to prove a hypothesis was registered before exploratory analysis saw the data). The SAP mentions 'Preregistration Ledger and in any published appendix' but the ledger is a document, not an artifact sealed by the platform. Without RFC 3161 timestamps and PROV-DM serialization, the audit trail is humanly-authored and vulnerable to post-hoc modification.
findings.evidence: src/state.py defines prov_activities as Annotated[list[dict], lambda a, b: a + b] (append-only). Line 51 comments: '# Provenance (W3C PROV-DM)'. rdflib>=7.0.0,<8 is in requirements.txt but never imported in src/. No agent calls any timestamp service. PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md is a template with sections '[To be populated]' (line 3: 'This document is a template. The SAP is finalized at Gate 2'). There is no code that appends to PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md or seals it with a timestamp. The state field prov_activities exists but is never populated by any agent. The infrastructure/observability/ setup includes Phoenix tracing, MLflow experiment tracking, but no RDF serialization or TSA integration.
findings.recommendation: Implement src/infrastructure/provenance.py: (1) Create a ProvenanceManager class that appends timestamped PROV-DM activity records to state['prov_activities']. (2) For RFC 3161 timestamps, integrate with a local or public TSA (e.g., http://timestamp.authority.example, or use a self-hosted RFC 3161 emulator for testing). (3) At Gate 2, before the split is finalized, serialize all prov_activities to RDF (JSON-LD format) and write to data/provenance/hypothesis_registration_TIMESTAMP.jsonld. (4) Sign the JSON-LD with the RFC 3161 timestamp. (5) Before Gate 2 approval, require that the preregistration ledger is sealed (timestamp + signature validated). (6) Document TSA configuration in RUNBOOK.md. Without this, the audit trail is aspiration; with it, the 'append-only PROV-DM' guarantee becomes enforceable.

findings.title: Reconciling LLM-as-Tiebreaker for Semantic Ambiguity vs. Deterministic Statistical Thresholds: Hybrid Decision Logic Undefined
findings.where: ADR-0013 (STRM comparison as secondary), P3 agent (genuine-vs-terminological convergence), SAP §3 (test families, Sensitivity analyses)
findings.problem: ADR-0013 frames P1 primary analysis as model-driven (cross-model agreement, silhouette score, anchor consistency) and STRM comparison as secondary. For P3 regulatory convergence, the 'genuine-vs-terminological distinction is a nuanced semantic judgment' (ADR-0016 Table 1, why P3 needs Tier 3 frontier reasoning). However, the SAP provides no decision logic for when LLM judgment overrides or modifies statistical findings. Example: If P1 finds 85% cross-model agreement on STRM '=' relationships but one model substantially disagrees, does the LLM (T3, hypothesis-formalizer) decide 'this is a genuine finding' or 'models agree, STRM is noise'? The SAP defers effect size thresholds and decision rules to Gate 2 after exploratory analysis (Section 3, Deferred). But the logic for integrating high-stakes semantic judgment (LLM) with statistical thresholds (confidence intervals, effect size) is not specified. This creates a gap between the research design (rigorously pre-registered statistical tests) and the execution (LLM judgment applied post-hoc during interpretation).
findings.evidence: ADR-0013 states 'All four outcomes are informative. None is a failure condition.' and 'Both high and low model-STRM agreement produce meaningful findings.' But SAP §3 (Sensitivity Analyses) is empty: '[List exploratory analyses planned before data examination. Additional exploratory analyses discovered during analysis are logged in lab_notebook.md with timestamps.]' There is no sensitivity analysis pre-specified that says 'if model-STRM agreement is <X%, we will consult LLM to assess whether STRM reliability is the issue vs. embedding model limitations.' P3 agent (agent.py) notes 'Terminological convergence classified and never reported as genuine' but provides no thresholding logic: is cosine similarity > 0.7 'genuine'? Is it decided by LLM annotation? The charter (P3 Hypotheses §13.2, §13.3) specifies H3.3 depends on LRF category (law vs. regulation vs. framework) but does not specify how LLM judgment is incorporated if the statistical test is inconclusive.
findings.recommendation: Before Gate 2, populate SAP §3 (Sensitivity Analyses) and §10 (effect size thresholds) with explicit decision rules: (1) For P1 model-STRM divergence: specify the minimum cross-model agreement (e.g., Spearman ρ > 0.60 on similarity scores across ≥3 models) before it qualifies as 'consensus.' Below threshold, STRM reliability assessment (inter-rater kappa on re-rated sample) is required before divergence is interpreted. (2) For P3 genuine-vs-terminological: define cosine similarity threshold (e.g., > 0.75 = genuine convergence; 0.50–0.75 = ambiguous, flagged for LLM review; < 0.50 = terminological only). (3) For all findings where LLM interpretation is applied, require that (a) the statistical test result is pre-specified, (b) the LLM is given the test result + confidence interval + any confounds, (c) the LLM's interpretation is recorded as an artifact with explicit reasoning, (d) the interpretation is never used to retroactively adjust the statistical decision rule. This preserves the exploratory/confirmatory firewall by ensuring LLM judgment refines interpretation (Tier 3 reasoning) rather than driving the statistical threshold (methodological choice).

findings.title: Null Results and Negative Findings Not Structured as First-Class Outputs: SAP Missing Decision Rules for Non-Significance
findings.where: SAP §2 (Secondary Analyses), §6 (Hypothesis Register), docs/charter/PROJECT_CHARTER.md (§5 Module Research Designs), 02_contribution.md
findings.problem: The GOAL states 'null results are first-class; this is TIER 1 of 3 — outputs must feed Tier 2.' The charter emphasizes 'Null results are structured identically to positive findings' and 'suppression of null and negative results is scientific misconduct.' However, the SAP is a template with empty sections for secondary analyses (§2), sensitivity analyses (§3), and confirmatory hypotheses (§6). No pre-specified decision rule exists for what constitutes a 'null result finding' vs. 'failure to reject the null due to underpowered analysis.' Example: H1.1 'STRM strength scores are positively correlated with embedding cosine similarity' has a null (ρ = 0). If the test is run on 280K mappings but finds Spearman ρ = 0.12, p < 0.001, is this a 'null result' (hypothesis rejected, genuine non-correlation) or a 'positive finding of weak correlation'? The charter does not specify how effect size thresholds interact with significance thresholds. The contribution statement (02_contribution.md) is a template with '[To be populated]' placeholders, so it is unknown what counts as a publishable finding at Tier 1 completion.
findings.evidence: SAP §2 is empty: '| SA-01 | | | | |'. §3 is empty: '[List exploratory analyses planned...]'. §6 Hypothesis Register lists the template format but no actual hypotheses. ADR-0012 states 'Publication happens when evidence is ready, not on an arbitrary schedule' and 'Readiness criteria (platform-evaluated): Minimum evidentiary threshold met for at least one primary finding; Null results documented with equal rigor to positive findings.' But the evidentiary threshold is unspecified. PROJECT_CHARTER.md §7 Cross-Cutting Requirements states 'Null results documented and published — a "no significant difference" finding is a finding' but the SAP does not include a pre-specified format for documenting null results (e.g., effect size, confidence interval, power analysis, interpretation of non-significance as evidence or just underpowered analysis).
findings.recommendation: Populate the SAP before data is examined with: (1) Per-hypothesis decision rule for effect size (minimum ρ, Cohen's d, F1 improvement required to claim significance). (2) Per-hypothesis power analysis and required N (from Phase 2.4 RESEARCH_DESIGN.md). (3) For each hypothesis, pre-specify the interpretation of three outcomes: (a) strong positive (effect > threshold, p < α), (b) strong null (effect < noise floor, p > α, power > 0.80, CI excludes meaningful effect), (c) ambiguous/underpowered (p > α but power < 0.80 or CI includes meaningful effect). (4) Populate 02_contribution.md with concrete examples of what tier-1 findings look like at the three outcome levels. (5) In the SAP, add a section 'Null Result Documentation Standard' that specifies the table format and text expectations (effect size + CI + power + interpretation of non-significance). This converts 'null results are first-class' from aspiration into operationalized contract.

findings.title: No Golden Standard / Holdout Validation Set for Evaluating Embedding Model Quality: Anchor Pair Circularity Acknowledged but Not Resolved
findings.where: SAP §12 (Limitations and Design Tradeoffs, Anchor Consistency Circularity), RESEARCH_DESIGN.md §2.5 (Evaluation Metrics), ADR-0013 (Phase 1/2 calibration sample evaluation)
findings.problem: The SAP acknowledges a fundamental methodological problem (Section 12, Anchor Consistency Circularity): 'The anchor consistency score uses STRM-asserted relationship pairs ('= Equal To', '∅ No Relation') as weak supervision. STRM reliability is simultaneously under investigation as part of the secondary analysis. This creates a partial circularity: STRM assertions are used to validate the embedding while their reliability is being tested.' The solution is incomplete: silhouette score and cross-model agreement are independent of STRM, but they do not directly validate embedding quality on GRC domain tasks. The Phase 1 benchmarks (STS-B, SICK-R) are 'coarse filter only' per SAP §12 (domain shift limitation), and the regulatory benchmarks (LexGLUE, ObliQA) have 'significant domain gaps' documented in platform.yaml. A human-labeled SCF control pair gold standard is designated as a 'post-publication community contribution milestone' (SAP §12, line 206). This means Phase 1 cannot definitively validate whether the selected embedding model captures GRC-relevant semantic relationships; the evaluation is circular (STRM validation) or domain-shifted (published benchmarks).
findings.evidence: SAP §12 lines 205–210: 'A human-labeled SCF control pair gold standard does not currently exist... designated as a post-publication community contribution milestone.' Platform.yaml regulatory benchmark section (lines 115, 129) notes 'domain_gap: full EU laws vs. 1-5 sentence SCF control descriptions' (LexGLUE) and 'financial regulation (ADGM) vs. cybersecurity GRC' (ObliQA). The P1 agent design (agent.py) does not call for expert validation of model output; it compares embeddings to STRM (secondary analysis) and internal metrics (silhouette, anchor consistency). No third-party validation is built into the pipeline. RESEARCH_DESIGN.md §2.5 specifies Evaluation Metrics per task but does not include 'gold-standard correlation' for the embedding selection task because no gold standard exists.
findings.recommendation: This is a methodological limitation that cannot be fully resolved without external resources. Mitigation options: (1) For Phase 1 scope: acknowledge that model selection uses domain-shifted benchmarks (STS-B/SICK-R for coarse filter) + structural metadata (silhouette, anchor consistency) and STRM comparison (with known reliability caveats documented in SAP §12). (2) For Phase 2 planning: allocate budget to recruit 5–10 GRC domain experts to hand-label 50–100 control pairs (highest-uncertainty regions identified by Phase 1 models) and compute inter-rater kappa. Use this as post-hoc validation of Phase 1 model selection. (3) Document the limitation in the final manuscript: 'Model quality was validated on domain-shifted benchmarks and internal metrics; a GRC-domain gold standard was outside project scope. A public domain-specific benchmark is a community contribution for future work.' This preserves scientific integrity (disclose the limitation) while acknowledging practical constraints.

result.lens: Architecture Assessment: Can the built LangGraph/governance system actually produce the P1-P5 empirical findings required to achieve the goal?

findings.title: Critical gap: No canonical data schema contract is implemented
findings.where: src/agents/base.py vs. Charter §3.1
findings.problem: The charter specifies Pydantic models (Control, STRMMapping, Framework, EmbeddingRecord, RiskCategory) as the 'contract every module depends on' (Charter §3.1).
